titles.title: Cost-effectiveness of pembrolizumab + chemotherapy versus cetuximab-containing regimens EXTREME and TPEx, for R/M SCCHN patients with a CPS between 1 and 19, in Italy

publisher.name: ISPOR - The Professional Society for Health Economics and Outcomes Research

dates.dateInformation: Presented at ISPOR Europe 2023, Copenhagen, Denmark

descriptions.description: This health economics poster presents a cost-effectiveness analysis comparing pembrolizumab + chemotherapy (P+CT) versus cetuximab-containing regimens (EXTREME and TPEx) for recurrent/metastatic squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck (R/M SCCHN) patients with a combined positive score (CPS) of 1–19, from an Italian National Health Service perspective. A three-state partitioned-survival model was developed using data from multiple clinical trials. Base case results show P+CT is dominated by both EXTREME and TPEx (fewer QALYs at higher cost). Across all scenario analyses, CCRs demonstrated cost savings.

Three-state partitioned-survival model (progression-free, progressed, dead). Efficacy from EXTREME, TPExtreme, GORTEC 2008-03, CheckMate-651, and KEYNOTE-048 trials. Survival extrapolation using AIC/BIC-selected parametric models. Italian NHS cost perspective including pharmaceutical (Farmadati), administration (National Tariff Nomenclator), and adverse event (DRG) costs. Probabilistic sensitivity analysis performed.

Presented at the Professional Society for Health Economics and Outcomes Research (ISPOR) Europe 2023, November 12–15, 2023, Copenhagen, Denmark. Poster number EE359. Sponsored by Merck (Funder ID: 10.13039/100009945).

sections.sectionContent: SCCHN is a group of cancers derived from the mucosal epithelium in the oral cavity, pharynx, and larynx. Treatment options for R/M SCCHN in Italy include cetuximab or pembrolizumab-based regimens in combination with chemotherapy. The EXTREME trial demonstrated improved OS for cetuximab plus platinum/5-FU. KEYNOTE-048 showed improved OS with P+CT vs EXTREME in total population and CPS ≥1 subgroup. Study objective: estimate CE of P+CT vs CCRs for R/M SCCHN patients with CPS 1–19 in Italy.

Three-state partitioned-survival model (progression-free, progressed, dead). Efficacy from EXTREME, TPExtreme, GORTEC 2008-03, CheckMate-651 trials for CCRs; KEYNOTE-048 CPS 1–19 data for P+CT. Survival models selected by AIC/BIC. Italian NHS cost perspective. Probabilistic sensitivity analysis performed.

Base case (vs EXTREME): P+CT dominated — incremental costs +€25,095, QALYs –0.428. Base case (vs TPEx): P+CT dominated — incremental costs +€11,993, QALYs –0.277. Scenario analyses ICER range: dominated to €437,830/QALY. KN-048 direct comparison ICER: €132,359/QALY vs EXTREME (above Italian WTP threshold of €60,000/QALY).

For R/M SCCHN patients with CPS 1–19, CCRs can represent more effective and cost-effective treatment options in Italy vs P+CT. Across all scenarios, CCRs demonstrated cost savings. Further research could establish more accurate relative effectiveness estimates.

imageCaptions.caption: Figure 1. Cost-effectiveness model schematic. Three-state partitioned-survival model showing Progression-free (PFS), Progressed (OS–PFS), and Dead (1–OS) states with survival curves over 10-year time horizon.
Figure 2. PSA scatterplots for base case analysis. Left: P+CT versus EXTREME; Right: P+CT versus TPEx. Both showing substantial spread with most iterations favoring CCRs on cost and QALY axes.

tableCaptions.caption: Table 1. Base case cost-effectiveness results: P+CT versus EXTREME. EXTREME dominates (more QALYs at lower cost).
Table 2. Base case cost-effectiveness results: P+CT versus TPEx. TPEx dominates (more QALYs at lower cost).
Table 3. Scenario analysis results for P+CT versus EXTREME and TPEx across alternative clinical trial data sources for CCR efficacy.
